data.title: Paladin Energy: Uranium Miner Is Still Attractive
data.description:
Introduction

Last year, I mentioned Paladin Energy (PALAF) (PDN:CA) appeared to be a good proxy to gain exposure to the uranium sector as the Australian company was restarting the Langer Heinrich uranium mine in Namibia. That was a timely article, as the share price (expressed in USD) is now up by approximately 60%. This is a good time to have another look at this uranium stock.

Selling two million pounds of uranium in a strengthening market

As the company’s financial year ends in June, it recently reported on its H1 results. Its flagship Langer Heinrich mine was able to sell 1.96 million pounds of uranium at an average realized price of US$70.5 per pound, for a total revenue of just over $138 million. This resulted in a gross profit of approximately $26 million as the operating expenses remained high during the ramp-up phase of the mine.

Also keep in mind Paladin is holding some uranium back in inventory, as the actual production numbers were approximately 17% higher at 2.3 million pounds, as a total of 2.36 million tonnes of ore at an average grade of 501 parts per million uranium were processed, with an average recovery rate of 89%. At the end of the first semester of its financial year, Paladin Energy had approximately 1.61 million pounds of uranium in its inventory. This inventory has a current fair value of approximately US$150 million.

Looking at the income statement, Paladin Energy established positive earnings in the final quarter of the calendar year. As you can see below, the total revenue in Q4 CY 2025 came in at US$102.4 million, resulting in a gross profit of just over $18 million. This ultimately resulted in a net profit attributable to the shareholders of Paladin Energy of $6.3 million.

While the quarterly EPS of US$0.014 isn’t really anything to get excited about yet, keep in mind the mine still has to reach its cruising speed. Mining activities are still ramping up, and the total production of 2.3 million pounds actually represented a guidance and expectation beat.

Paladin has learned from the mistakes it made in the past and now runs a very liquid and cash-rich balance sheet. As you can see below, the company ended December with $121 million in unrestricted cash and just over $157 million in short-term investments. Additionally, the finished products have a book value exceeding $120 million (but the market value is substantially higher, as the 1.61 million pounds of finished products are in the books for just $88 million).

This results in a positive working capital position of almost $370 million, while the total debt position (excluding lease liabilities) came in at $152 million. A debt level that’s comfortably covered by the cash and short-term investments.

Advancing Patterson Lake South

The healthy balance sheet also allows the company to continue to invest in its other assets. The Patterson Lake uranium project in Canada was added to its development portfolio after completing the acquisition of Fission Uranium. Subsequent to the end of the first semester, Paladin Energy received the state ministerial approval of its Environmental Impact Statement following a review period. This is an important piece of the puzzle to get the Patterson Lake South project.

As a reminder, the Patterson Lake South project is a very high-grade uranium deposit, as it hosts a stunning 130 million pounds of uranium within a 3.3 million-tonne resource at an average grade of approximately 1.8% across the categories. This includes a mineral reserve of 3 million tonnes, at 1.41% for a total of 94 million pounds. As per the technical studies that have been completed on this project, PLS could produce just over 9 million pounds of uranium per year at an all-in sustaining cost of less than $20/pound.

And while the initial capital expenditures to bring this project into production are estimated at in excess of $1.2 billion, the strong production numbers and low production cost still result in an IRR of almost 30% at $90 uranium.

The timing for PLS could work well: once Langer Heinrich reaches a steady-state production rhythm, the cash flows from the Namibian mine in combination with the existing liquidity on the balance sheet should be sufficient to fund the equity portion of the PLS construction capex.

Investment thesis

Although Paladin’s share price has increased by roughly 60% since my previous article, I still think the stock is worth a "Buy" rating, although a "buy on weakness" during a temporarily weaker uranium market would likely be the best course of action. The current share price is relatively high based on the Langer Heinrich mine, but if and when the PLS project starts up and produces almost 10 million additional pounds of uranium per year at an industry-low all-in sustaining production cost, that’s where the company will make the real money.

The combination of production and development makes Paladin Energy an intriguing uranium idea for investors that would like to have exposure to this energy metal.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: March Madness Dividends: Why We Are Betting On The Rebound
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

It's March, and for some people, that means it's time to spend a lot of time watching basketball. In the spirit of the sport, we are going to talk about a couple of "rebound" picks.

With investing, as with all aspects of life, not everything goes perfectly. The best-laid plans rarely survive contact with reality. Whether you are trying to shoot a 3-pointer or you thought you had an easy layup, sometimes things are a little off.

Yet when things go wrong, that doesn't mean that it is over. You grab the ball on the rebound and find a way to score. Today, we're going to look at two companies that both ran into tough times, but they grabbed the ball and are looking to generate strong returns for shareholders in 2026.

Pick #1: ACRE – Yield 11.5%

Ares Commercial Real Estate Corporation (ACRE) had a rough 2025; at one point, it was down 40% for the year before starting to recover. While the tough year was shared among peers, ACRE saw the biggest sell-off.

Coming into 2026, to our members, we wrote this:

"ARI is currently ahead of the others in this process, with BRSP close behind. ACRE seems to be a few quarters behind, which is likely why it has underperformed the most.

In terms of dividends, we are confident that ARI and BRSP will comfortably cover their dividends. ACRE has a path to cover its dividend, but it is less certain. We could see dividend raises from ARI or BRSP in mid-to-late 2026. We believe ACRE will regain coverage but will keep its dividend flat for 2026."

We didn't have to wait long. ACRE announced earnings, and we are pleased to report that they have regained dividend coverage for Q1 with $0.15 in distributable earnings. However, there is a caveat as they realized a $2 million gain on the sale of a property they previously foreclosed on.

The most important thing to keep an eye on is the size of the portfolio. ACRE's earnings declined from 2023 to 2025 because its leverage declined. A larger portfolio produces more cash flow. We've seen ACRE's peers start leveraging up early in 2025, and ACRE has been a bit late to the party. However, we did see them increase leverage in Q4 2025: Source

In the earnings call, management said they are targeting getting their portfolio up to a 3.0x debt/equity ratio, which would be $1.5 billion in debt and a $2 billion portfolio.

"Jeffrey Gonzales, CFO & Treasurer

Yes. I think a simple way to look at it just going back to earlier in the call, is just looking at if we get to our 3.0 ratio that were our target, you're looking at a $1.5 billion of debt and about a $2 billion loan portfolio size. So I would say that's the easiest way to look at it."

In order to get there, ACRE needs to continue resolving the loans it holds that are in default. ACRE has 5 loans that are in their highest risk ratings – 4 & 5. However, in terms of capital, $270 of the $316 million (85%) is tied up in two properties:

Freeing up capital from those two properties will go a long way to allowing ACRE to rebuild its loan portfolio. The Chicago office building has an occupancy of 90% with an average lease term of 8 years, while the Brooklyn condo is being marketed, and ACRE will be paid for each condo unit sold. While the pace at which these properties will be sold is unpredictable, there is a significant amount of value to be unlocked, and it is very plausible that it happens in 2026.

This is why ACRE was up double-digits yesterday, as investors are starting to see the light at the end of the tunnel, and it is increasingly likely that the dividend will be maintained through 2026. A dividend raise could be on the table in 2027.

ACRE remains a buying opportunity. Book value is $9.26, and we believe that the current discount is much larger than is reasonable since book value already includes a substantial CECL (Current Expected Credit Loss) reserve. We believe a 20% discount to book value would be appropriate, which would be around $7.40/share.

Pick #2: MAC – Yield 3.4%

The Macerich Company (MAC) is a mall REIT that is in our "hold to sell" portfolio. This means that it isn't paying the yield we target, but we have decided to hold and wait for the price to get to fair value before exiting. For those who are at their portfolio income goals, MAC is an option to seek capital gains and dividend growth.

The recovery hasn't been in a straight line in terms of share price, but they have been executing their plan consistently quarter-to-quarter.

Occupancy has been a key challenge for mall REITs in general for nearly a decade now. Before COVID, the retail sector was struggling, and malls were seeing rising vacancies, and COVID didn't help.

MAC's occupancy in their "Go-Forward" Portfolio, the properties they intend to keep, is up to 94.9%—up from 94.6% last year: Source

MAC has completed about $1.4 billion in asset sales, and its 5-year plan is targeting $2 billion. In 2026, they will continue to focus on leasing, which is now more focused on getting new tenants into the spaces they agreed to lease.

MAC has identified tenants for all 30 of its anchors that are being replaced. They expect to open 7 anchors in 2026 and another 12 in 2027. Source

As these anchors take physical occupancy, rent will increase, and that will help MAC hit its targeted NOI from its Go-Forward Portfolio of $860-$900 million, up 17-23% from 2025's results. If MAC achieves its targets, we expect the company to be trading north of $30 by 2028. They remain on track to hit their goals and are making more progress every quarter.

Conclusion

We frequently recommend that investors have a minimum of 42 different holdings in their portfolio, because we know that not everything works out. By having a diversified portfolio, we don't have to panic when things go wrong. We can identify which holdings are worth waiting for the rebound, and which aren't—making the decision based on our assessment of the fundamentals.

Both of these companies have started their rebound and are materially higher than the lows they saw during their respective difficulties.

They are on the path to recovery, which will provide shareholders with rising share prices. We don't expect any dividend raises in 2026, but dividend raises could be on the table in 2027.

With ACRE, we expect the price to have some upside to close the discount to book value to a more reasonable level. Overall, we expect the dividend to continue to make up the majority of the total return. For MAC, your return is likely to be much more weighted toward capital gains, as we expect them to keep the dividend minimal until they finish their portfolio alignment in 2028.

We expect both investments to provide strong returns in 2026.

data.title: American Eagle Outfitters: Buy The Dip As Aerie Drives Surging Comps
data.description:
The mood in the stock markets in early 2026 is decidedly shaky, and it wouldn't be an overstatement to say that investors are panic selling at the slightest trigger. A number of high-quality businesses are being ignored and dumped, even in spite of strong results in the Q4 earnings season.

American Eagle Outfitters (AEO), the teen-focused clothing brand, is one of those names. The company has seen a resurgence in relevance ever since a pair of powerful ads featuring Sydney Sweeney and Travis Kelce, which have injected the brand with product newness not seen in years. In spite of this, the stock remains in bear market territory, with shares down ~30% since the start of January (though we do note American Eagle is still up ~50% over the past year). Recently, in early March, the company posted strong Q4 results and a robust outlook for FY26, nevertheless triggering a ~5% post-earnings selloff. It's an opportunity, in my view, to survey the damage and buy the dip.

I last wrote a buy rating on American Eagle in January, when the stock was trading at $26 per share. Since then, American Eagle has dropped ~30%. While I acknowledge that the timing of my buy call was premature (I certainly didn't predict the macro bearishness that would emerge as the "SaaSpocalypse" narrative built up steam, or the geopolitical tensions that have dominated headlines over the past few weeks), I continue to see a vibrant business here that is defying retail sector weakness to gain market share. I reiterate my buy rating here.

The first element that we should discuss is the fact that American Eagle issued a strong outlook for FY27 (the year for American Eagle ending in January 2027). In spite of this, the stock's sharp selloff beginning in January has made its forward multiples look even more appealing. My core portfolio strategy amid volatile times is to keep my head down and continue investing in high-quality businesses at a discounted price, while still gearing up for near-term volatility. Given the strength of American Eagle's recent trends, I have no issues dollar-cost averaging my position down and waiting for sentiment in the stock market to turn.

As shown in the chart above, American Eagle is expecting mid-single-digit comp sales in FY27. This is building off an already-tough comp in FY26: as reminder, American Eagle's comps started accelerating in Q3 once the impact of the Sydney Sweeney jeans ad took off. Importantly, management is expecting strong holiday sales trends to carry over into Q1, which is expected to clock in at a high single-digit comp.

But the bottom line indicators here are also important. American Eagle expects to deliver gross margin improvements, which is a signal of strong merchandising and inventory management in the wake of a full-year headwind from tariffs. The company is expecting $390-$410 million in GAAP operating income, the $400 million midpoint of which translates to 77% y/y growth (yet still slightly below $427 million in operating income in FY25).

Assuming relatively minor contribution from net interest income and other income items for the year, and a 26% tax rate that is flat to FY26, post-tax net income would be $296 million. Divided by 177 million weighted average shares, the company's EPS would be $1.67 (+49% y/y).

This positions American Eagle's valuation at just 10.9x FY27 P/E. When we look even at the battered retail landscape, which has decimated many peer stocks, we find that American Eagle's valuation still sits nicely below many core peers:

In my view, given American Eagle's recent acceleration in comp sales (which is not at all the norm in a retail industry battered by choppier consumer spending), the stock should be able to return to previous valuation levels at a mid-teens multiple of earnings. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through American Eagle's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

American Eagle's revenue grew 10% y/y to $1.76 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $1.74 billion (+8% y/y) by a two-point margin. Revenue accelerated four points versus 6% y/y growth in Q3.

Core to the company's recent improvement in growth has been its comp sales trends. In fact, comp sales (which, as a reminder, measures the revenue growth from existing store locations, without including the contribution of newly opened or recently closed stores) grew 8% y/y, which is a sharp acceleration from 4% y/y growth in Q3 (which, in turn, accelerated from a -1% decline in Q2). To me, it's not hyperbole to say with this substantial turnaround in results that American Eagle is having its moment in the spotlight, with a strong holiday sales quarter to prove it.

We note as well that this isn't necessarily common across the industry. To me, American Eagle's closest direct peer is Abercrombie & Fitch (ANF), which in Q4 decelerated to just 1% comp sales growth (down 2 points sequentially from 3% in Q3).

Core to American Eagle's sales strength is its intimates brand, Aerie. Aerie has enjoyed a surge in recent popularity. Net sales for Aerie in Q4 grew 27% y/y to $683.8 million, or 39% of the company's overall sales (rising from a 34% share in the year-ago Q4).

Per CEO Jay Schottenstein's remarks on the Q4 earnings call regarding the company's recent sales surge:

We were thrilled to see the remarkable momentum at Aerie and OFFLINE, which delivered 23% comp growth. Robust demand was broad-based across categories and channels. By leveraging our stronger market position and heightened demand, we exited the quarter with record brand awareness. And customer acquisition was up in the double digits. With successful expansion underway across a number of categories, we see significant runway to continue to build Aerie and OFFLINE and capture new audiences in the years ahead.

I'm also pleased by the consistent and steady progress we've seen at American Eagle. Comps grew 2%, accelerating from the third quarter with growth across genders. Product initiatives are delivering more newness and fresh trends right collections. Following impactful partnerships with Sydney Sweeney and Travis Kelce, Martha Stewart's holiday campaign, reinforce AE's cross-generational appeal as the ultimate gift-giving destination. Customer counts and retention rates are proof points of success."

The company is also outperforming on the bottom line as well. Gross margins lost only -30bps y/y to 37.0%, despite the company citing $50 million (284bps) of tariff headwinds in-quarter.

Pro forma EPS of $0.84 also grew 54% y/y, and beat Wall Street's expectations of $0.71 with 18% upside.

Key takeaways

In my view, American Eagle remains a solid company with improving comp sales, which makes it an outlier in a rather shaky consumer macro environment. Despite its relative strength, we find American Eagle to be an excellent bargain at just an ~11x forward P/E ratio. Stay long here and use the irrational dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Vital Farms: The Egg Story Isn't Broken
data.description:
Vital Farms, Inc. (VITL) has become a sore spot in my portfolio. My two buy ratings are both largely in the red. The stock was most recently hammered following their most recent earnings. The stock saw its price drop by nearly 20% in a single day and is now down by 35% over the past year.

The company showed the first signs of cracks in its egg growth story in the latest earnings report. The company beat revenue estimates but missed on the earnings. The company had beat earnings estimates in 15 straight quarters until the latest Q4 result. It also lowered guidance for the year.

Despite the negative news, the company did still show strong growth. It also maintained its long-term revenue and profit targets. I understand why the company saw some selling pressure based upon the quarterly results. I think the selloff was overdone and has pushed the company to a point where it is undervalued.

Financials

The company continued to show growth when it reported its results. But it did fall short of earning estimates and lowered revenue guidance going forward. The company reported revenues of $213.6 million for the quarter, good for growth of 28.7% for the year. The revenue actually beat estimates, but the earnings fell short. The company reported EPS of $0.35 versus the $0.39 expected. This was growth of more than 50% from the prior year. Adjusted EBITDA also increased significantly year over year, going from $19.1 million to $29.2 million. So the growth story is still intact, with revenues growing by nearly 30% and earnings by over 50%. The concern in the quarter is that the company has a history of beating earnings, not falling short.

The other negative aspect of the report that helped cause the stock drop was the weakened guidance. The company provided a revenue estimate of $900 million to $920 million, which is below the estimate of $938.6 million. The company lost approximately $200 million in market cap. I don’t think a decrease of $18.6 million in the revenue estimate warrants that kind of drop. The company also forecast EBITDA of $105 million to $115 million for 2026, which would be flat compared to 2025. I think the bigger concern for investors is the story of consistent growth and results that is now being put into question. I know it is one of the strengths that I have seen in the company. Management consistently gets results and beats estimates. This is the first sign of that consistent growth story being put into question.

On that topic I will say I think that the company is being conservative. When brought up on the earnings call, management noted that they wanted to get ahead of the slower start they have had to the year and adjust expectations now. Quoting the CFO, “We just set expectations very clearly at the beginning. And then we keep our pattern of beating expectations that we set at the beginning of the year.”

One of the other strengths that is continuing to play out is the operating leverage for the company. Costs continue to decline as a percentage of revenue. Management noted during the earnings call that they expect this to continue to be the case going forward. The company has done a great job of keeping its costs under control as it expands and grows the business. The management team has done an exceptional job in my mind. It is one of the reasons I like the stock. The company did see an increase in marketing expenses. That is expected to remain slightly elevated from where it was last year. There have been multiple issues, such as capacity constraints, bird flu, etc., that have caused the company to spend less on marketing. I think taking the operating leverage and using part of that to go back into marketing is smart. You have to keep awareness up and bring in new customers.

Capital Allocation

The company has always done an exceptional job on their balance sheet and capital allocation. The company still does not have any debt. It ended the quarter with $113.4 million in cash. This is after the company has been in expansion mode and adding new manufacturing lines and facilities. That cash on hand is over 12% of the market cap of the company currently. That is a strong cash position, especially for a company that is growing and expanding.

The company has been in expansion mode. The company finished its new line at their ECS facility during Q4 last year. This leaves them with three production lines in the facility. It also leaves them with approximately $1.2 billion in egg revenue annually. The company is also still in the process of building out their new Seymour facility, called Vital Crossroads. This facility is expected to be operational in early 2027. To date, the company has been able to fund all expansion with cash. As part of the expansion to complete Vital Crossroads, the company is expecting CapEx to be $140 million to $150 million. It does not have enough cash on hand to complete the project, but between cash on hand and future cash flows, the company should have sufficient cash to not have to take on any debt if it does not want to.

The company introduced a share buyback plan during the earnings announcements. The board approved a $100 million share buyback to be utilized over the next two years. I think this is a great option made by the board. The company has a history of strong profit and cash flow. With the current spend on their new facility, that cash flow has been tied up, but come 2027, the company should return to strong free cash flow. The company is expecting another good EBITDA number in 2026 and should have cash for both the buybacks and building the new facility. If it wants to get aggressive, the company could tap into its revolving credit line. Management noted that they will look at this as an option. I think this share buyback is driven by two main factors. The company has strong cash flows, and with the large capex expected to be complete, they can use some of that to return to shareholders. The other is that the share price has taken a very large drop. It is to a point where management and the board think there is value in buying shares. $100 million is more than 11% of the current market cap of the company. I personally hope that the company gets very aggressive at the current stock price to repurchase a large amount of shares, utilizing the revolving credit line if needed.

Valuation and Outlook

I originally invested in Vital even though it had a higher valuation due to the growth and strong performance of the company. After the most recent price drop, I think that Vital has turned into a value play more so than a growth play, although the growth is still there.

The company currently has a market cap of $891 million. It just ended the year with revenue of $759.4 million, leaving a PS ratio of 1.17. It is also projecting revenue of $900 million to $920 million, leaving the forward PS ratio less than 1. For a company that is growing revenues at over 20% a year, that is an extremely low PS ratio. Also for a profitable company. Companies that are operating at a loss or low margins can see lowered PS ratios, but Vital is neither of those. The PE ratio currently sits at 13.8 as well; that is well below the median.

Those cheap valuations don’t take into account the cash position of the company either. If we look at enterprise-level metrics, then the company looks even cheaper. Overall, I see the company as a value play based upon the value multiples applied to the company. It is priced as if it is going to see minimal growth or even lose ground. I don’t see either happening. The company projects 20+% growth in 2026 and going forward.

Longer term, the company has provided guidance that it will reach $2 billion in revenue in 2030. That is 5 years of operating from now. While the company did have a slight miss on its earnings this quarter, company management has been great at forecasting and hitting its internal targets. While 2030 is a long way off, the history of management has me much more comfortable with this estimate. The company needs to grow revenues at approximately 21.4% a year to achieve its goal of $2 billion in revenue. I think that is a reasonable number that the company can achieve. It grew revenues by over 25% last year. It has projected revenue growth of 22% on the high end for 2026, which I think it will beat based upon management commentary. It will be much harder to hit that growth rate in the later years as the base becomes much larger, but this does not seem like a goal that is out of reach.

If the company is to trade at the same P/S metric in 2030 as now, the company would be looking at a valuation of $2.34 billion. That is a 167% increase from today's market cap. That is a solid return over a 5-year period. We can also discount this future value to get an idea of the current market cap. I ran the PV calculation with a discount rate of 12% and 15%. A 15% discount rate is pretty high; I would not typically apply that high of a rate to a profitable and growing business that has been as consistent in its results as Vital, but if there is implied risk in the company achieving that future revenue figure, then we can use a higher rate to gauge value. I think a 12% rate is more appropriate for this use case.

Even at a higher rate of 15%, the company is still being undervalued by 30%. That, of course, is based upon the company hitting its $2 billion goal. It is also assuming that the market does not give it a higher PS multiple. In my opinion, the current multiple is low. It can be assumed that the market has already taken the cash position and buyback plan into account in the current multiple, although once again I do not think it has at this point.

One other way to look at this is to calculate the future value of the current market cap of $891 million with a 12% rate. We will use the same P/S ratio of 1.17 for the calculation. At a 12% discount rate, the company would have a future value of $1.57 billion, which equates to a revenue number of $1.35 billion in 2030. That is an average revenue growth rate of 10% each year. I don’t think there is any chance that the company only grows revenue at 10% each year for the next 5 years.

Overall, yes, the company has a strong forward goal of $2 billion. Based upon historical growth rates, this seems like an achievable goal for the company. Based upon the current value, it appears that Mr. Market does not trust management to be able to achieve that goal. There are multiple ways for me to look at the valuation and come to the same conclusion: the stock is undervalued.

Conclusion

Vital Farms showed the first cracks in its egg growth story. The company missed earnings estimates, while still hitting revenue estimates, and also lowered guidance for 2026. The stock had a steep sell off following the report. The miss was small, and the revisions to the revenue estimate were also small. The drop was overdone and has left the company in a position that I consider undervalued.

The company is still showing strong growth and profitability. The company continues to maintain its long term growth trajectory, and it is currently on track to meet the estimates. Management has been very good about providing consistent results. I do not think the slip in the current quarter is a future trend. I expect the company to hit and most likely exceed its estimates for the year, based upon the company history. While the stock has been a poor performer in my portfolio, I used the latest sell-off to add to my position. I think the sell-off was overdone and has created a value stock that is priced at no growth, while growing at over 20% annually. I think the stock is undervalued and rate it a buy. The market is volatile right now, but in the long run, Vital Farms is a buy.

data.title: NNN REIT: 3 Reasons Why It's Still A Buy
data.description:
NNN REIT (NNN) is one of the cornerstones of my dividend portfolio - I'm a long-oriented investor with a strong tilt towards income-generating assets. That's why REITs have always been one of my favorite ways to invest.

I don't think I have to tell you - not all REITs have been created equal (or should I say managed equally?).

Throughout the years, I've reviewed numerous REITs, and several have caught my attention for standing out from the rest. Regardless of the criteria you prefer to look at - income potential and stability, business metrics, valuation, track record, or business model - truly top picks perform strongly from many perspectives.

I think NNN is such a case. It has many qualities, and a few of them can be easily ranked as "the best" in the entire sector - that's why I've been bullish on NNN since the first time I covered it.

But I'm not going to sugarcoat it. NNN is not as attractive now as it was months ago. Don't get me wrong, I still think it can be a worthy addition to a well-structured portfolio, but those who bought when I recommended it last time managed to secure a better price.

Since the last time I covered NNN, its stock price has increased by over 13%, delivering a total return of 15%.

Now, I see that NNN has published its quarterly data for Q4 2025. On top of that, I saw a dynamic upward movement in its stock price, and these are the reasons why I decided to prepare a follow-up coverage. I will present you with three key reasons why I still consider NNN buyable.

Three Reasons Why NNN Is Still A Buy

Great diversification in a highly attractive business model

I always like to look at the diversification from a few perspectives, namely: tenant structure, industry exposure, and geography. I think NNN has one of the best portfolio structures from the perspective of diversification. Firstly, I see that no tenant represents a share in ABR higher than 5%, with the largest one responsible for 4.3%. Secondly, I see that NNN has exposure to over 35 lines of trade, and while they are mostly focused (nearly 35% of ABR) on automotive services and convenience stores, I consider both these fields fragmented and defensive in nature.

I could say the same about NNN's geographical perspective. I see that NNN is present in 50 states and leases its properties to 400 tenants. I'm pasting some details below from NNN's presentation for Institutional Investors.

I think diversification is important for REITs as they rely on recurring cash flow from leased assets. I feel much more comfortable with a REIT that has 20 tenants responsible for 46% of ABR, which is the case of NNN, than with a REIT that has 1 tenant responsible for such a large portion of ABR. I think that makes sense to all of us, it mitigates the risk of any unpredictable issues or even defaults across tenants.

Top-tier business and credit metrics

Each time I review a REIT, I search for some key metrics that help me understand how the business is going. I will discuss some of them here, and I will state right from the get-go: I think NNN performs very well across the most important KPIs. That indicates a healthy business model to me.

Despite owning nearly 3,700 properties, I see that NNN has always kept its occupancy in check. As of Q4 2025, it amounted to 98.3%, which is coherent with its historical average. What's most important to me is that NNN's occupancy has never fallen below 96.4% despite many market disruptions (e.g. financial crisis or COVID-19).

What I like to see across REITs are long-term oriented lease structures. That's why I'm satisfied with the effect of NNN's negotiations with tenants. NNN is generally capable of securing 10-20 years of lease terms, and I see that its WALT (weighted average lease term) amounted to 10.2 years as of Q4 2025. I think that's especially important with a triple-net lease structure, as it includes contractual rent increases (with most of the costs covered by the tenant), so I can see a predictable and growing rent roll for years ahead.

Balance sheet strength is very important across all businesses, but I pay special attention to this aspect when analyzing REITs. Their entire business model is structured in a way of acquiring financing (debt and equity) to acquire and lease properties. But I have no concerns in terms of NNN, as I think its balance sheet is second only to Realty Income (O) in this market segment.

As you can see in the chart above, NNN has a well-structured debt maturity schedule. To be honest, I think that "well-structured" is an understatement. NNN has the best debt maturity schedule of all REITs in the market, as its average maturity term amounts to 10.8 years. On top of that, I see that NNN has a 4.14x fixed charge coverage and ~40% debt in the financing structure, which makes me even more confident in NNN's ability to service its debt.

Improving investment volume sets the stage for more dynamic AFFO per share growth

I was very satisfied to see NNN's investment volume recover from $565m recorded in 2024. In fact, I see that NNN reached a record investment volume in 2025 and it amounted to $931m. I think it's nice to see that most of NNN's acquisitions have been realized through relationships, especially that NNN has been able to secure higher cap rates through its relationships (7.4% vs auction-related 7.1%).

As the management commented during the last Earnings Call:

As outlined in this morning's press release, NNN delivered a solid operating and financial performance in 2025, generating 2.7% growth in AFFO per share and completing over $900 million of acquisitions, the highest annual volume in NNN's history. The momentum exiting 2025, driven by elevated acquisition activity and the portfolio management of the vacancies, positions NNN well entering more uncertain macroeconomic environment in 2026.

Yes, the AFFO per share growth wasn't impressive last year, but the management shared somewhat more dynamic guidance for 2026:

We are establishing an AFFO per share guidance range of $3.52 to $3.58 and core FFO per share guidance of $3.47 to $3.53. The midpoint of our AFFO range represents 3.2% year-over-year growth in 2026, accelerating from 2.7% growth in 2025 as we move past the tenant issues experienced in late 2024.

It's not all sunshine and rainbows

I realize that NNN is not the best pick in the market. I have two reasons for it. Firstly, I think the valuation is not as attractive as it used to be, especially given my reason number two: relatively slow growth.

I will start with the valuation. The last time I covered NNN, it traded below 12.0x. Now, it trades at nearly 13x forward-looking P/FFO, which is still quite modest, but I think it's crucial to understand the context.

I like comparing businesses to their peers. In this case, I will compare NNN to Agree Realty (ADC). While NNN intends to grow its AFFO per share by 3.2%, ADC has a target of 5.4%. I consider this 220 bps a substantial difference, especially given a comparable size of both REITs (based on their market cap).

I feel like NNN's valuation has increased generously given its still relatively slow growth prospects.

Also, NNN also has a risk of another issue with tenants, and I always like to remind myself that despite the recent actions of the Fed, interest rates could take a turn depending on the market environment. No investment is risk-free, and this applies to NNN as well, even despite its defensive nature.

Despite These Negatives, NNN Could Be A Buy

I will summarize my thoughts quickly. I consider it one of the best-quality REITs available in the market. NNN operates in a highly attractive and defensive market niche, and I think its management is doing a very good job. I judge that by NNN's business and credit metrics, which are at a top-tier level, especially given occupancy, lease and debt schedules, and fixed charge coverage.

Valuation-wise? Yes, it's not as attractive as it used to be. It is lower than ADC's or O's multiples, which suggests to me that it could be an opportunity. But I'd like you to remember one crucial thing. Despite a similar size to ADC and a much smaller size than O, NNN expects a relatively slow AFFO per share growth.

Still, I think NNN has a place in a well-structured portfolio oriented around income. It has one of the longest and most reassuring dividend histories among all publicly traded REITs. I see that NNN currently trades at ~5.3%, which I consider quite attractive given its stable nature and predictable cash flows.

That's why I can declare NNN a buy, but I will say this again - I'm closer to a hold than I used to be a couple of months ago. If the stock price growth continues, I will review this again and turn my Buy rating to a Hold.

data.title: The Tape Is (Rapidly) Deteriorating: Markets Are Weak, Breadth Is Terrible
data.description:
As I'm writing this piece in the pre-market session of March 6, it all points out that the three major U.S. benchmarks, the S&P 500, Nasdaq 100, and Dow 30, will be finishing the week in the red. Interestingly enough, all three are also down over the past month and the past three months.

While I agree with the consensus that the conflict in the Middle East dominated the pressures this week, I argue that the market's weakness was already building up prior to the start of Operation Epic Fury on February 28.

In fact, I have been aggressively moving to cash since mid January, and I recently initiated a (highly) speculative short position, betting on a 10% correction in the markets in the first half of the year.

In this piece, I review the tape this week, and I discuss my thoughts on what to monitor in the next weeks, beyond the war in Iran. To be clear, I reiterate that, by the end of the year, any near-term correction will be just noise. Fundamentals are strong enough to support a continuation of the bull run once sentiment revives, likely in the back half of the year.

The Market Tape

Let's start with benchmarks.

Last week, the S&P 500, the Nasdaq 100, and the Dow were down, as noted by the proxy ETFs that mirror these indices.

This time, investors used the Iran war as the excuse to head for the exit. I already discussed my thoughts on this war in this piece. Long story short, I expect a near-term correction in the S&P 500 of approximately 10% in the first half of the year (which I'm betting on with put options), followed by a V-shaped recovery as retail and the Street buy the dip.

What! Why would they buy the dip?

First, fundamentals (especially in AI-exposed stocks) are mostly intact, as evidenced by Nvidia's earnings last week and, more recently, Broadcom's and Marvel's strong guidance.

The latest FactSet report shows strong sequential earnings growth in tech, communication services, and even in financials (despite the nervousness around private credit).

Affordability remains a problem, with the broader consumer discretionary sector down 0.9% yoy in Q4 2025 (so far, as there are still 205 companies to report Q4 earnings). Given the recent increase in fuel prices at the pump (see below), I think this sector will continue to be under pressure in Q1, which is one of the reasons why I'm shorting the XLY with put options.

Second, in anticipation of the mid-term elections, I strongly believe the newly nominated Fed Chair, Kevin Warsh, will cut down interest rates (aggressively).

As I noted in an earlier coverage, I wouldn't be surprised to see a jumbo cut in June, just to catch up with Powell's slowness (and to please Trump, who nominated him as Fed Chair, of course).

Please remember that back in June last year, Trump floated the idea that the Federal Reserve should cut the benchmark interest rate to 1%. One percent!

Yes, I know, I know. Trump says a lot of things and changes his mind quickly (remember his tariff agenda between April 2 and April 9, 2025). I don't really care if the target is 1%, 2%, or 2.5%. What I care about is the directional move, which clearly seems to be dovish from today's 350 -375 bps rate. With the mid-term elections on November 3, I think Warsh has a lot of catch-up work to do once he takes over from Powell in May.

Of course, this puts the Fed’s independence at risk. Still, I think Wall Street (and retail) will welcome any rate cuts and look past the Fed's independence. On the surface, lower rates would reduce government borrowing costs and help the economy. However, inflation still needs to stay in check, especially after the January headline PPI came in hotter than expected (0.5% m/m vs the consensus of 0.3% m/m). Furthermore, I see some nervousness around Warsh’s quantitative-tightening stance, which could temper some of the excitement around rate cuts. To be clear, cutting down interest rates and shrinking the Fed’s balance sheet go in opposite directions, so I wouldn't be surprised to see some volatility in H2 as well (skewed to the upside, of course).

At the moment, the market is not pricing in a single rate cut until the September meeting. I reiterate: I strongly believe the new Fed Chair will surprise the consensus below, which could be the catalyst for the V-shaped recovery.

As for the recent Iran war "selloff" (I mean, come on, we're down barely low single digits), I think the markets would have been down this week anyway.

Why?

Prior to the war in the Middle East, both the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100 were on a neutral trend as the tech rotation unfolded.

Up until this week, the Dow Jones (see 1-year chart below) and the Russell 2000 were the beneficiaries of this rotation, but the recent hike in oil prices is likely to pressure some of the key constituents within these two indices.

In fact, if we look at the last month and the last 3-month returns, most U.S. benchmarks are all in the red, except the Russell 2000's 3-month return, which greatly benefited from the rotation out of large caps:

Speaking of the rotation out of tech, despite some hopes this week, largely driven by retail buying the dip in beaten-up software stocks, the sector is still the second-worst performer in terms of 3-m returns. Naturally, on red days like today (note that I'm writing this in the pre-market session of March 6), tech takes the largest hit.

Before I conclude the section, I just wanted to share with you the anecdote that I mentioned above about investors buying the dip in software. This industry was the top-performing theme in tech last week, up 5.3% (market-cap-weighted return among S&P 500 constituents only).

Looking Ahead

Bottom line, I expect a 10% correction in the SPY in H1 this year, followed by a V-shaped rally in H2 (Q3 rather than Q4). Why a 10% correction?

Astrology... otherwise known as technical analysis. For the few chartists reading this piece, I'm joking, of course.

From the chart above, 610-620 seems to be a decent support, and I don't expect the current pressures to lead to a bigger than 10% correction in the broader markets.

From an economic data perspective, I would keep a close eye on next Wednesday's February CPI report at 8:30 a.m. ET. The consensus is looking for 0.2% m/m for headline CPI and 0.2% m/m for core CPI. Given the nervousness after the hot January headline PPI, I think the market will pay very close attention to this release. To be clear, I don't expect to see a major spike in energy inflation in the February CPI, given that the conflict in Iran started on the last day of the month. Gasoline prices didn't have enough time to jump in just one day.

As for tariffs, after the U.S. Supreme Court ruled on February 20 that Trump lacked authority under IEEPA to impose his global tariff agenda, Trump announced a new 10% global tariff under Section 122. This week, Scott Bessent said that this rate will be raised to about 15%.

Overall, I see inflation worries back on the radar.

Speaking of inflation, Brent oil is another chart I would closely monitor next week. As I'm typing, Brent is trading for $88/bbl, despite the fact that the consensus for 2026 is roughly between $55–$65/bbl due to the oversupply, a topic I discussed in this article. What I find interesting is that Brent is up after news from Washington that the U.S. is now exploring several measures to stabilize oil prices, including the possible use of the Strategic Petroleum Reserve. Given that the disruptions affect Asia more than the U.S., China is also pressuring Iran to allow the passage of its own ships through the Strait of Hormuz.

So far, I don't think it makes sense for Iran to de-escalate. It is my view that Iran may want to further escalate the tensions in the Strait of Hormuz to strengthen negotiating leverage, then pivot to talks later. I think that's why the country is targeting foreign ships in the region, with the latest one being a tanker at anchor off Kuwait on March 5, adding the total number of commercial ships hit since February 28 to 9.

From a market breadth perspective, in the last month, the breadth in most of the 11 sectors has been quite poor and very dispersed, except for energy (obviously, given the oil risk premium), utilities, and real estate:

For reference, the vertical axis of each chart above represents the breadth (i.e., the number of stocks with positive returns in the last month, from 0% at the bottom all the way to 100% at the top), and the horizontal axis represents the returns (with 0% in the middle of the chart). Each dot is an industry within the sector, with its size proportional to the market cap of its constituents.

Overall, this weak and mostly uneven breadth across the sectors is reinforcing my view that the market had already been losing momentum before the geopolitical headlines in the Middle East accelerated the downward move. Even though I remain bullish long-term, I warrant caution in the current U.S. markets, especially in the first half of the year.

data.title: Pricing The AGI, Top 3 Stocks For AI-Energy Alpha
data.description:
In my opinion, Wall Street consensus is currently mispricing the Artificial General Intelligence [AGI] revolution by treating it as a software and silicon scaling. What I see mostly in this is an ontological failure. To answer why? As the market enters 2026, the AGI build-out has fastly shifted into a thermodynamic and electrical physics asymptote. The market is assuming infinite compute elasticity, as I see Eaton's proprietary U.S. Dodge data center tracking that indicates a terminal physical bottleneck. The U.S. data center construction backlog is now at 206 Gigawatts [GWs] that indicates an 11-year duration at 2025 build rates.

But the industry technically cannot deploy 206 GWs of accelerated compute using legacy 480-volt AC architectures and traditional cooling without going beyond issues of entropy and grid capacity. The theme for the Picks is the Arbitrage of AI Energy Physics. To capitalize on the next decade of AGI shift, smart bulls should rotate from the silicon layer (my perspective) into the Top 3 stocks for AI Energy Alpha: Base-Load Generation (CEG stock), Voltage−Step Topology (ETN stock), and Entropy Management (VRT stock).

Constellation Energy Corporation Stock (CEG)

The main catalyst for Constellation Energy stock is the $22 billion Calpine acquisition and exclusive edge on generation. AGI training clusters are intolerant of grid intermittency. Meaning they need a 100% capacity factor. Constellation Energy, post the $22 billion acquisition of Calpine (January 2026), has changed into an irreplicable 55 GW energy apex asset (the largest private-sector power producer). I am focusing on the pricing-in of the volumetric synergy of this merger into CEG stock. By combining a 22 GW nuclear fleet operating at a 94.7% capacity factor + Calpine’s 23 GWs of dispatchable natural gas + geothermal (730 MW Geysers) + 800 MWs of battery storage, Constellation Energy has built a moat for hyperscaler Power Purchase Agreements [PPAs].

Moreso, the point of selection on the list is the financial architecture of Constellation Energy′s long−term sovereign tech contracts. The Crane Clean Energy Center (835MW) restart is backed by a 20−year Microsoft PPA and a heavily $1 billion DOE Loan Guarantee fixed at an ultra-low spread of just 37.5 basis points above U.S. Treasuries (maturing Oct 2055). At the same time, the Clinton 30 MW uprate is locked by a 20-year Meta PPA.

Apart from that, Constellation Energy's existing nuclear fleet is protected from downside commodity price risk through the Inflation Reduction Act's (and the 2025 OBBBA's) Nuclear Production Tax Credit [PTC] that is providing a transferable credit up to $15/MWh. As a result, it is protecting Constellation Energy's revenue until gross receipts hit the $26 to $44.75/MWh phase-out threshold. Overall, Constellation Energy holds the baseline needed for the 206 GW data center pipeline.

Eaton Corporation Stock (ETN)

For Eaton stock, the upside catalyst here is the 800V DC Transition and $9.5 billion Boyd Thermal Integration. As rack densities scale toward 1 megawatt to back NVIDIA's (NVDA) Rubin Ultra and Feynman architectures, traditional 480-volt alternating current [AC] distribution marks a physical copper and busbar limit. Eaton is actively shifting the industry towards 800-volt Direct Current [DC].

For more clarity, there is a 5% efficiency advantage. Current data center topologies have a ~5% electrical loss stepping down medium-voltage utility AC feeds to 54-volt rack DC. Eaton's integration of Resilient Power Systems ($86 million acquisition) supports medium-voltage solid-state transformers to convert utility feeds directly into DC. So, on every 100 GW national build-out, Eaton's architecture targets 5 GWs of stranded power from the grid.

More on this, Eaton is executing an aggressive horizontal integration strategy. By acquiring Boyd Thermal for $9.5B (closing Q2-2026), Eaton is targeting the microchannel cold-plate architecture born from aerospace engineering. This drives Eaton's accessible TAM per data center megawatt from $2.9 million to $3.4 million. Eaton’s Electrical Americas Q4-FY2025 orders grew 16% on a 12-month rolling basis. Along with that, data center orders up ~200% Y/Y and Backlog expanded by 31% Y/Y to $13.2 billion with a solid margin profile for 2026.

Vertiv Holdings Stock (VRT)

The bullish catalyst for Vertiv stock is the 45°C Fluid Dynamics Reality and the $1.138 billion PurgeRite acquisition. The recent market panic regarding NVIDIA’s CES comments that indicates next-gen chips can run on warmer water (45°C) and thus eliminate chillers, is a misinterpretation of thermodynamics. My point is that warmer water allows for more free cooling, but rejecting heat 24/7/365 in non-Arctic climates needs mechanical assistance during peak ambient temperatures. VRT has solved this with the Trim Cooler. This is a hybrid large dry cooler paired with a smaller mechanical chiller.

Focusing on the moat, pumping ultra-clean, chemically stable liquid through 100-MW campuses is beyond an IT task. In my opinion, it is heavy-industrial fluid management. Vertiv’s $1.138 billion acquisition of PurgeRite gives them the single-seller state on secondary fluid network commissioning, flushing, and lifecycle maintenance. On this, Vertiv’s Q4-FY2025 financial trends are important to back my argument. Organic orders accelerated ~252% Y/Y, and that is pushing the book-to-bill ratio to 2.9x. The backlog more than doubled to $15 billion (+109% Y/Y). Vertiv brought-in $910 million in Q4 adjusted FCF (+151% YoY) with an operating margin of 23.2%. The catalysts make Vertiv a tollbooth for preventing thermal shutdown at the rack level.

The Bear Case For The Picks For AI-Energy Alpha

Eaton is deploying $1.5 billion into 24 capital projects to scale up Electrical Americas capacity. Adding manufacturing headcount and depreciation ahead of the sales ramp forms friction. Eaton may have a 130 basis point margin headwind in 2026, front-loaded in Q1/Q2. More so, Eaton is executing the spin-off of the $3 billion Mobility unit by Q1-2027, and this is a big operational distraction, in my opinion. On the positive, Eaton's structural pricing power overrides this. It maintains a 30% margin in Electrical Americas despite these ramp costs, and can have $13–$13.50 Adjusted EPS for FY2026.

On Vertiv's side, it may be forced to increase CapEx to 3%-4% of sales in 2026 (up from historical 2-3%) to fund brownfield expansions in the Americas and greenfield sites in Asia. If the 12-to-18 month backlog delivery timeline faces construction delays from the client side, Vertiv's working capital cash inflows (that popped to $1.004 billion operating cash flow in Q4, largely based on advanced milestone payments) can reverse into a liquidity drain, in my stance.

Lastly, Constellation Energy has a massive $12.9 billion Nuclear Decommissioning Asset Retirement Obligation [ARO]. This ARO is highly sensitive to discount rates. I mean, a 50 bps decrease in CARFR increases the ARO by $125 million. More so, to satisfy the DOJ settlement for the Calpine merger, Constellation Energy must divest 6 power plants by September 4, 2026. A forced fire-sale in a tight regulatory window can lead to an unfavorable recovery of capital.

Conclusion

All in all, the AGI infrastructure shift is beyond a software play as it is an aggressive mobilization of electrical physics and thermodynamics. CEG stock is the anchor with the 55 GW baseload needed to power the 206 GW pipeline backed by federal PTC floors and 20-year hyperscaler PPAs. Whereas, Eaton stock has the efficiency arbitrage with the 800V DC power routing and the $3.4 million/MW wallet share expansion through the $9.5 billion Boyd Thermal integration. Vertiv stock is linked with hyper-growth based on a $15 billion backlog, 252% order growth, and the inescapable fluid dynamics (PurgeRite and the Trim Cooler). I see that Wall Street is playing musical chairs with trillion-dollar silicon valuations, but kind of ignoring the caps of the physical grid. In my opinion, to own the silicon stock is to speculate on the algorithm, but to own CEG, ETN, and VRT stocks is to own the thermodynamics basing the algorithm itself.

data.title: February Jobs Report: Signs Of Slowdown, But Rate Cut Unlikely
data.description:
Recent geopolitical stresses have raised the risks of a slowdown, as an extended oil price shock can't be ruled out. The latest labor market report, only supports the possibility.

In a jobs market that hasn't even been impacted by the US-Iran war yet, the latest developments aren't positive. In fact, they suggest that the first signs of some slowing could well be here, which in turn can be exacerbated by the ongoing tensions. Here, I look at what the report says, followed by what it means for monetary policy in the context of the larger situation and the best investing options.

What the labor market report says

In February 2026, unemployment rate inched up to 4.4%, taking it back to December 2025 levels, after dropping to 4.3% in January. This number isn't a matter of real concern, though, considering that the Fed targets exactly this rate for 2026.

The real story in the latest jobs report is the changes to non-farm payrolls, which were a shocker. The figure dropped by 92k, a sharp turnaround from a 130k increase in January 2026, as per the initial estimate. While there's some comfort to be gained from the fact that the drop is partly explained by strikes in the healthcare sector, it's not enough. Only losses of 28k were seen on this count, so 70% of the drop in payrolls is still unexplained.

A closer look at the data across industries reveals early signs of a slowing down in the economy. These are supported by revisions to past data and bigger picture trends over time. The details are as follows:

  • Cyclical cooling off: Industries like manufacturing and construction, as well as leisure and hospitality, have reported job losses of over 10k each. Since these are cyclical sectors, the latest numbers could well indicate a coming slowdown.
  • Downward revisions: Payroll figures for December 2025 have been revised downwards quite dramatically. As against the initial estimate of 48k job gains, 17k jobs are now estimated to have been lost during the month. For January 2026, the revisions were better, but the additions were still reduced by 4k to 126k.
  • Average additions see sharp drop: The bigger picture shows job losses in five of the last 12 months (see chart below). The government shutdown in October 2025 makes the numbers look worse, but that's just one month. In any case, even excluding it, the average additions to non-farm payrolls over the past 12 months is ~27k. By comparison, for the 12 months ending February 2025, the average additions were at 89.3k.

Rate cut probability drops

Just going by the labor market data, it would appear peculiar that the probability of a rate cut has reduced since I last checked following the CPI print for January 2026. At the time, only a 9.2% likelihood of a rate reduction existed already. The number is now down to just 3.7%, as the chart below shows.

But then this probability needs to be considered holistically. Escalating stresses between the US and Iran have been a key development in recent days. The macroeconomic impact is already becoming obvious, as, among other things, it has led to dizzying increases in crude oil prices.

WTI crude is up by 38% since the war began at the end of February. Crude price is at USD 91/bbl right now, the highest since July 2022, when it had risen due to Russia's invasion of Ukraine and supply chain challenges following COVID-19.

If the situation continues, an increase in inflation is on the cards, which in turn can affect growth. But keeping inflation in check will be, as has been the case historically, the Fed's top priority. And that, in turn, reduces the likelihood of a rate cut.

Stock market response

The stock markets were understandably unhappy with the labor market report, with the three key indices all losing value in yesterday's session. The NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX) (QQQ) saw the biggest drop of 1.5%, followed by the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, SPY), which was down by 1.3%, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) dropped by around 1%.

Where to invest

Sectors like energy and defense have been big gainers this year, which is a no-brainer. A 27% rise in the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) YTD has been witnessed, while the Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF (PPA) is up by 15%.

They can bring further short-term gains, but that raises the ethical challenge of profiting from war. Instead, considering defensives like consumer staples and utilities might be a good idea now. Based on the latest labor market report and risks to inflation, they too can offer short-term gains.

Among the two defensive sectors, utilities, as seen from the State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU), looks better placed. It's rated "Buy" by Seeking Alpha analysts, although Quant Rating still gives it a "Hold." But it's still better off than the State Street Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Inc. ETF (XLP), which gets a Hold across both rating types. To me, this suggests there could be single stocks, or XLU peers that have more convincing ratings and can make better buys.

What's next?

The latest labor market report is yet another reminder of how fast investing scenarios can evolve. Just last month, I made a case for cyclicals. Now, there's one for defensives. In the relatively short term, of course.

What has remained unchanged, however, is the market view that no rate cuts will happen when the Fed meets later this month. Even if the jobs report would have made a case for easier monetary policy, the risk to inflation just rose too significantly to ignore.

If geopolitical stresses subside soon enough, though, the focus could go back to growth, which doesn't look good as per the latest labor market report. This too can change, but that remains to be seen.

data.title: Camp4 Therapeutics: Strong Cash Runway As CMP-002 Moves Toward Human Trials
data.description:
Thesis

Camp4 Therapeutics Corporation (CAMP) has just reported an FY25 GAAP EPS of -$2.65, a figure that misses analyst expectations by about $0.77. We’re seeing continued losses typical of where they are as a clinical-stage biotech with a hefty R&D spend. Revenue, on the other hand, was in line with estimates at $3.5 million, which was about a 438.5% year-over-year increase, but still only minimal and was just some collaboration-related income. The big bonus here was that they managed to end the year with $109.5 million in cash, up from $64.0 million back in 2024. Management gave a decent runway expectation, and with the upcoming milestones in 2026, this is going to be important. Now, Camp4 has a lot of early promise in its lead candidate, CMP-002, and I think we could see upside for the stock based on clinical milestones for this candidate over the next couple of years, as I'll explain.

Camp4 Therapeutics FY25/4Q25 results

If we take a look at the financials, Camp4 hinted at several strategic developments going on that boosted their liquidity even though they’re going through a pretty capital-intensive period. They managed to end FY25 with about $109.5 million in cash and cash equivalents, a significant jump from the $64.0 million we saw back in 2024. Now, this was mainly due to their financing activities; we saw an oversubscribed private placement providing about $50 million in upfront funding with the potential for another $50 million later on. There was also a $30 million underwritten equity offering, as well as that $17.5 million collaboration payment from GlaxoSmithKline (GSK). As a result, the balance sheet is looking far stronger, and management has tipped for a cash runway extending sometime into 2028.

Operationally speaking, research and development expenses stayed pretty flat at $38.2 million compared with $38.8 million in 2024. So there is continued investment in pipeline programs going on, particularly CMP-002 targeting SYNGAP1-related disorders; however, there were some reductions, especially with CMP-001 and a lease modification gain, which slightly offset costs. Elsewhere, general and administrative expenses increased to $17.4 million, up from $14.9 million last year, which I think we can put down to higher professional/corporate support costs we usually see with small biotechs scaling operations and managing collaborations.

Overall, they reported a net loss of about $80.4 million, a lot more compared to the $51.8 million loss in 2024. But keep in mind, this bigger loss can, for the most part, be put down to a $29.8 million non-cash accounting loss. This was related to the fair-value adjustment of derivative tranche liabilities from the September financing. Revenue was pretty minimal at just $3.5 million, which was really just some early collaboration revenue. So I think it’s safe to say that Camp4 is showing signs of transitioning toward clinical development here. Liquidity is up significantly, and research costs are being kept in line. So overall, the company is in a decent position to initiate that global Phase 1/2 clinical trial for CMP-002 as early as the second 2H26, while still being able to explore partnership opportunities for CMP-001.

Current pipeline

Just as a quick overview of the pipeline, Camp4 is pretty focused on regulatory RNA-targeting antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) therapeutics. These would be designed to increase gene expression and thus restore deficient protein levels in some genetic diseases. As you know, their lead candidate, CMP-002, targets the SYNGAP1 gene for the treatment of SYNGAP1-related neurodevelopmental disorders. Currently, it’s in late preclinical development with GLP toxicology studies underway, but the company has plans to start a first-in-human Phase 1/2 clinical trial as early as the 2H26. Elsewhere, they have also developed CMP-001 for urea cycle disorders. Again, focusing on increasing protein levels, this works by upregulating CPS1 expression to help restore urea cycle function. This program actually entered Phase 1 clinical testing; however, they’ve since paused further internal investment and are instead exploring partnership opportunities to continue development. Now, beyond just these two programs, CAMP4 is advancing some very early discovery-stage candidates in the central nervous system and metabolic diseases. This is where they have some collaboration, including one with GSK, which is for the discovery of additional RNA-based therapeutics targeting genes, specifically those involved in neurodegenerative and kidney diseases.

CMP-002 up to now

Since CMP-002 would be the big near-term catalyst for Camp4, it's worth taking a deep look at its development. It’s a first-in-class ASO therapy tipped to target the underlying genetic cause of SYNGAP1-related neurodevelopmental disorder. This is a pretty severe condition in which we see intellectual disability, epilepsy, and developmental delay. Mechanistically speaking, the CMP-002 job here is really quite simple on paper. It works by targeting regulatory RNAs that control the transcription of the SYNGAP1 gene. So by using Camp4’s RAP platform to identify the specific regulatory elements governing gene expression, the ASO can then bind to these regulatory RNAs and amplify the transcription of the SYNGAP1 gene. This should then increase messenger RNA production with the ultimate end goal of raising SYNGAP protein levels in neurons. And because SYNGAP1 disease is caused by haploinsufficiency, in which we only see one functional copy of the gene, leading to about a 50% reduction in protein levels, even a very modest increase in expression levels here could meaningfully improve synaptic function and thus neurological outcomes. As a therapy, the current design is an injection into the cerebrospinal fluid to reach the central nervous system directly.

So far, the preclinical results supporting CMP-002 have been rather encouraging. In humanised SYNGAP1 haploinsufficient mouse models, we’ve seen that a single dose restored SYNGAP protein levels close to normal and also produced a dose-dependent increase in protein expression across brain regions. Even some experiments showed up to about a 1.7-fold increase in protein levels. What’s more impressive is that the treated animals also showed some improvements in disease-relevant behavioural phenotypes. There was better motor performance, reduced hyperactivity, and some improvements in learning/memory tasks. On top of this, treatment of non-human primate studies also demonstrated that repeated intrathecal dosing was pretty well tolerated and produced significant increases in the SYNGAP protein. Again, this was across multiple brain regions, which would suggest adequate CNS distribution and pharmacodynamic activity. This latter part here is a pretty important translational signal for human trials.

Global Phase 1/2 clinical trial for CMP-002

So right now, Camp4 is conducting some GLP toxicology studies required for regulatory approval. They aim to start clinical testing soon with plans to launch a global first-in-human Phase 1/2 trial as early as 2H26. I think we will see the trial follow a typical rare-disease ASO study design. So first we will have dose-escalation cohorts to evaluate safety/tolerability, and pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics after intrathecal administration. The next step would be expansion cohorts to assess preliminary efficacy in patients. At this stage, the key endpoints I’ll be looking out for will be safety and tolerability, particularly with respect to CNS adverse events, which can be a major roadblock for some gene therapy programs.

Of course, we want to see biomarker evidence of increased SYNGAP protein expression or downstream signaling changes. Eventually, early clinical signals like improvements in seizure frequency, cognitive function, or neurodevelopmental measures would potentially be a massive outcome for Camp4. Now, since SYNGAP1 is a monogenic disorder with pretty clear mechanistic biomarkers, even if we see just modest increases in protein expression or improvements in EEG or behavioural endpoints, this could actually be considered proof of concept. As for a timeframe, we are still very early in development, but I think if the study begins in late 2026, early safety and biomarker readouts could realistically emerge sometime in 2027. Keep this timeframe in mind before investing, as Camp4 has very little in terms of upcoming clinical catalysts, and we may see stock volatility based on earnings, before shares build good momentum on the back of clinical readouts. The more meaningful clinical efficacy signals would potentially appear much later, with 2028 as a rough timeframe, but this depends heavily on enrollment and follow-up duration. This would also represent the first human validation of Camp4’s regulatory RNA-targeting platform, so it's quite a big step forward in the right direction.

CAMP stock valuation

As for a fair valuation of Camp4 stock, it currently trades at a market cap of about $238 million with an enterprise value of just $169 million, because of the company’s decent cash balance relative to just minimal debt. And since Camp4 is still a clinical-stage biotech without any real commercial products, traditional profitability metrics aren't that meaningful, so we would be better served to use revenue/balance-sheet multiples here. In that sense, the EV to sales multiple sits at 44.5x and the price to sales at 25.6x. Now, both are far above the healthcare sector medians of roughly 3x to 4x sales, but keep in mind the company only generated about $3.5 million in revenue. So these multiples would appear expensive on a purely financial basis, but it’s pretty typical for where the company is at.

The market is mainly pricing Camp4 on its pipeline potential. They hold a price-to-book ratio of 3.7x, which is somewhat closer to sector norms, but also reflective of that strengthened balance sheet after the recent financing and collaboration payments. Overall, the strategic progress of Camp4’s RNA-targeting platform and the advancement of CMP-002 this year would make up the valuation potential here. There should also be some upside from its collaboration with GSK, which investors are pricing in.

Looking ahead

Looking ahead, the long-term value in Camp4 stock here is to build momentum on the back of CMP-002. It’s both a rare disease, and we typically see a high value assigned to disease-modifying genetic therapies. As for a brief TAM estimate, SYNGAP1-related disorder is estimated to occur in about 6.1 to 10 people per 100,000 individuals; however, far fewer people have actually been formally identified worldwide, with Camp4’s estimate being just over 10,000 patients in the U.S. So it would indicate substantial underdiagnosis, and a potentially larger addressable population could exist here with genetic testing expansion. This, of course, places CMP-002 in the category of ultra-rare neurological disorders, in which we usually see very small patient populations supporting meaningful commercial opportunity. We should also note here that there are currently no approved disease-modifying therapies for SYNGAP1 on the market. The current treatment only consists of supportive interventions such as behavioural therapy and seizure management. So it means the competitive landscape for Camp4 here is relatively open, since most efforts are still in early research or preclinical development.

From a commercial viewpoint, investors usually benchmark rare genetic therapies against other one-time or disease-modifying gene/RNA therapies. We usually see these command hefty prices between roughly $850,000 and $3 million per patient, depending on durability and clinical benefit. So early-stage trials for gene therapy can take on quite a bit of stock momentum as a result. If CMP-002 does ultimately demonstrate meaningful increases in SYNGAP1 protein expression, investors could begin modeling a rare-disease therapy with pricing in the high six-figure to multi-million-dollar range, but keep in mind it is still very early on. So, to recap, the key catalysts over the next few years will be initiation of the Phase 1/2 trial and enrollment. Then, early biomarker confirmation of SYNGAP1 protein upregulation. As the company is in a pretty good position cash wise to support the trial, with a lot of the equity raising already done, I do see more upside for this stock over the next couple of years.

data.title: ARK Innovation ETF: The Trading Strategy Is Now Flashing 'Sell' (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My most recent article on the ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK), titled "Time To Go All In" and published in October 2025, deserves a meme thumbnail (see below). As it turns out, the timing of my bullish report almost perfectly matched the stock's peak price level achieved since the beginning of the inflation and interest rate shock of 2022, at $93 per share. From there, the ETF declined by about 18% over less than five months, while the S&P 500 (SPY) remained largely flat.

While I deserve to (and will gladly) poke fun at the poor timing of my article, I think that the trading strategy that I discussed last year deserves a closer inspection. Rather than considering it a failure because of the double-digit losses produced recently, I think that the "sell" signal that it is now flashing should be taken seriously by those who buy into the idea of holding the fund for long-term price appreciation while cutting the downside to prevent sizable losses.

On the Fundamentals of ARKK

Before diving into the trading strategy, let me address the fundamentals of the ARK Innovation ETF.

The fund, largely speaking, seeks long-term gains from exposure to the technologies of the future: electric and autonomous vehicles, represented by ARKK's still largest holding, Tesla (TSLA), at 10.3% allocation; digital currency, expressed through the likes of Coinbase (COIN) at 4.8% allocation, which is a bit lower than the 6% as of early October; gaming and media, among others (see allocation chart below). The ETF is large at an AUM of $6.7 billion, liquid at an average daily trading volume of 8.5 million shares, and charges a hefty management fee of 75 bps.

My views on ARKK's investment philosophy have not changed in the past half-year. I still believe that, strategically, the approach makes sense and has a decent chance of outperforming, over the next couple of decades, bets in "old economy" sectors like oil and gas, industrials, etc.

But tactically, I continue to have the same reservations as before. First, the ARKK "quasi-black box" model allows for quite a bit of portfolio management discretion when choosing what to invest in and how much. Worse, the team's track record is iffy at best. The ETF has massively underperformed the Nasdaq 100 index (QQQ) since inception, other than during the "buy everything" melt-up of the earlier COVID-19 days that was driven by extra-loose monetary and fiscal policies—see the chart below.

Second, I also dislike Cathy Wood's trademark idea of trimming stock positions when valuations rise and accumulating shares when valuations dip, which is another way of saying, "Keep the losers, ditch the winners". This approach is consistent with either value investing or short-term tactical allocation that aims at benefiting from cyclical swings in prices. However, it is inconsistent with long-term investing in secular growth stories, in my view.

On the Trading Strategy

A quick look at the ARKK price chart (above) will probably make it clear that, at least historically, the ETF has displayed trending behavior and high volatility. I believe that the latter is disruptive and pretty much always detrimental to a portfolio's risk-adjusted performance.

However, the former can be an asset for investors who are diligent at riding the upside of a robust growth story while trimming losses before they become catastrophic. This is what is at the core of my ARKK trading strategy of owning shares while the 50-day moving average is above the 200-day moving average and then selling shares when the opposite is true.

Following this approach wouldn't have prevented an investor from incurring the 18% losses of the past five months. But for a very volatile ETF like ARKK (36% annualized volatility since the 2014 inception, which suggests twice the risk of investing in the Nasdaq 100), such a dip should be considered "business as usual". Investors who disagree should probably stay away from ARKK altogether.

The chart below shows that the two moving averages are now crossing for the first time since June 2025, with the 50-day line heading lower this time (worth noting, between the buy signal of June 2025 and today's sell signal, ARKK climbed 14% from $65 to $74, precisely matching the absolute returns of the S&P 500). This is the sell signal that might prevent investors who still believe in the long-term potential of ARKK from riding the roller coaster further down.

Lastly, I should point out that going all in or all out on ARKK does not mean betting a substantial portion of one's portfolio on this ETF, regardless of loss-mitigation tactics that might be deployed. Since the end of 2019, for example, ARKK has produced an annualized volatility of 47% and a maximum drawdown of 81%, while the trading strategy would have trimmed both to 33% and 48%, respectively. Still, despite the stop-loss triggers, these risk metrics are quite high for most investors, so diversification is highly advisable.

While individual risk tolerance and the characteristics of a portfolio's other holdings should dictate how much one might want to allocate to ARKK, I think that anything above 10% is speculative. Regardless, today, my trading strategy says that exposure to this ETF should head to zero and stay there until ARKK finds its footing once again.
